title: Grouped Convs as a Middle Ground
concept: The number of groups G is a single dial: G=1 gives you a standard convolution where every channel mixes, G equal to the channel count gives you a depthwise convolution where no channel mixes, and every value in between is a grouped convolution.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's the main cost of using more groups in a grouped convolution?

options.text: Cross-channel mixing shrinks, since each group can only combine its own slice of channels

exercises.explain: Right — more groups means less mixing between channels. Today: let's see just how far that dial can turn.

cards.title: One number, G, controls everything
cards.body: You've been treating G as just 'the number of groups.' But G is really a single dial. Turn it one way and you land on something very familiar. Turn it the other way and you land on something else you've already met.

cards.title: The two extremes of G
cards.body: Set G = 1: there's only one 'group,' containing every input channel — that's exactly a standard convolution, full mixing. Set G = (number of input channels): every group holds exactly 1 channel — that's exactly a depthwise convolution from Unit 101, zero mixing. Any G strictly between those two extremes is what this unit calls a grouped convolution.

cards.title: A single mixing-board slider
cards.body: Picture a mixing-board slider with as many channels as your CNN layer. Push the slider all the way to one end and every channel gets blended into one mix — that's standard conv. Push it all the way to the other end and every channel stays on its own separate, untouched track — that's depthwise conv. Every stop in between groups a few channels together while keeping other channels apart.

cards.title: 8 channels, four settings of G
cards.body: Take a layer with 8 input channels. G=1: 1 group of 8 channels — standard conv, everything mixes. G=2: 2 groups of 4 — grouped conv. G=4: 4 groups of 2 — still grouped conv, tighter groups. G=8: 8 groups of 1 channel each — depthwise conv, nothing mixes at all. Same 8 channels, four completely different amounts of mixing.

cards.title: Depthwise isn't a separate invention
cards.body: It's easy to think of depthwise convolution as a totally different mechanism from grouped convolution. It isn't — it's the exact same grouped-convolution mechanism, just pushed to its most extreme setting, where every group happens to contain only one channel.

cards.title: The full spectrum, G=1 to G=channels
cards.body: One dial, two landmarks at its ends — everything in between is exactly what this unit calls a 'grouped convolution':

left.text:
One group, all channels
Full cross-channel mixing
Most weights

right.text:
One channel per group
Zero cross-channel mixing
Fewest weights

exercises.prompt: A convolution layer has 16 input channels. If G=1, what kind of convolution is this?

options.text: A standard convolution — every filter mixes all 16 channels

exercises.explain: Right — G=1 means a single group containing all 16 channels, which is exactly a standard convolution.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a depthwise convolution is a completely different operation from a grouped convolution, unrelated in mechanism.
exercises.explain: False — depthwise convolution is simply the extreme case of grouped convolution where G equals the number of input channels.

exercises.sentence: Setting G equal to the number of input channels turns a grouped convolution into a ___ convolution.
exercises.explain: Correct — one channel per group is the definition of a depthwise convolution.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly places grouped convolutions on the spectrum between standard and depthwise convolutions?

options.text: G=1 is standard (full mixing), G=channels is depthwise (no mixing), and every G in between is a grouped convolution
Grouped, standard, and depthwise convolutions are three unrelated operations with no shared structure
Depthwise convolution always uses more weights than a standard convolution

exercises.explain: Exactly — one mechanism, one dial (G), sliding between two extremes you already know.

exercises.prompt: A layer has 32 input channels. An engineer sets G=32 for its convolution. Another engineer, on a different layer with the same 32 channels, sets G=1. What kind of convolution does each engineer end up with?

options.text: G=32 gives a depthwise convolution (1 channel per group); G=1 gives a standard convolution (all channels mixed)
Both settings produce the exact same standard convolution, since G doesn't affect the mechanism
G=32 gives a standard convolution and G=1 gives a depthwise convolution — the reverse
Neither setting is valid; G must always be strictly between 1 and the channel count

exercises.explain: Correct: G=32 puts every channel in its own group — depthwise. G=1 puts every channel in one shared group — standard. Next: a look at grouped convs' surprising history.
